title: 2 Chronicles 24
subtitle: Joash — Good Under Jehoiada, Evil After

sections.header: Verses 1‐14 — Temple Repair Under Jehoiada’s Guidance

refs.note: Both accounts record the temple repair, but they differ in details. Kings says the priests initially failed to repair the temple; Chronicles says Joash was the driving force and introduced a collection chest.
The 'tax that Moses the servant of God laid on Israel' (v. 6) refers to the half-shekel atonement money from Exodus — Joash revived an ancient Mosaic institution to fund temple restoration.

notes.note: The principle demonstrated here — that God’s governance is consistent and consequential — applies to every generation. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Chronicler’s distinctive vocabulary shapes the presentation, connecting this section to his broader theological framework. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Joash repairs the temple, echoing 2 Kings 12. The Chronicler’s version emphasises Jehoiada’s role: “Joash did what was right in the eyes of the LORD all the years of Jehoiada the priest.” The qualification is ominous — faithfulness depends on the mentor’s presence. Jehoiada dies at 130, buried “in the City of David among the kings” — a priest honoured with royal burial.

heb.paragraph: Bayit (house) carries a deliberate double meaning in Chronicles: Solomon builds God a house (temple), and God promises David a house (dynasty). The interplay between these two 'houses' generates the theological tension that drives the entire narrative: will the dynasty endure? Will the temple remain? Both depend on covenant faithfulness.

notes.note: The temple construction represents the pinnacle of Israel's theocratic kingdom — God dwelling visibly among His people. Yet this physical temple was always a shadow of the greater reality: Christ Himself is the true temple (John 2:19-21), and believers collectively form the living temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 3:16).

The glory of the temple — its furnishings, priests, and sacrificial system — all point beyond themselves to the Person and work of Christ. The altar speaks of His cross; the veil of His flesh (Heb 10:20); the Holy of Holies of direct access to God through Him alone. No physical temple can contain the infinite God (Acts 7:48-50), but God graciously condescends to dwell among His people.

For the New Testament believer, the application is profound: we are now the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 6:19-20). The care, reverence, and holiness that characterized temple worship should characterize our daily lives. We are called to present our bodies as living sacrifices (Rom 12:1) — holy, acceptable to God, which is our reasonable service.

sections.header: Verses 15‐27 — Apostasy After Jehoiada; Zechariah Murdered; Joash Assassinated

refs.note: Kings records Hazael's threat and Joash's assassination but omits the apostasy and Zechariah's murder. Chronicles adds the devastating spiritual collapse after Jehoiada's death — the king who restored the temple later desecrated it.

Jesus names 'Zechariah son of Berekiah, whom you murdered between the temple and the altar' — likely referencing this Zechariah (son of Jehoiada). His dying words — 'May the LORD see and avenge' — become a bookend with Abel's blood.

Luke's parallel places the murder 'between the altar and the sanctuary,' matching the Chronicles account precisely and confirming that Jesus saw this event as representative of Israel's pattern of killing prophets.

notes.note: Calvin: On 27 — Apostasy After Jehoiada; Zechariah Murdered; Joash Assassinated: teaches that all human religious activity is ultimately provisional — when God himself appears, our best efforts are suspended in awe. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 27 — Apostasy After Jehoiada; Zechariah Murdered; Joash Assassinated: Gen 22:2. Jewish tradition universally accepted this identification, which became foundational for Jerusalem’s theology of sacred space. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: After Jehoiada’s death, the officials lead Joash astray. Prophets are sent but ignored. Zechariah son of Jehoiada prophesies against them; Joash has him stoned in the temple courtyard. Zechariah’s dying words: “May the LORD see this and call you to account.” Within a year, Aram invades with a small force and defeats Judah’s large army “because Judah had forsaken the LORD.” Joash is assassinated in bed. “He was not buried in the tombs of the kings.” The man the temple saved becomes the man who desecrates it.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew milḥāmâ (war) in the Chronicler's theology is never merely military. Every battle is a theological event: victory comes from trusting God, defeat from trusting human alliances. The Chronicler consistently adds 'the LORD fought for them' or 'they relied on the LORD' to battle narratives that Kings presents in purely political terms.

notes.note: The record of royal wickedness serves as a sober warning that privilege and position offer no immunity from the consequences of sin. Each king who 'did evil in the eyes of the LORD' demonstrates that departure from God's Word leads inexorably to moral and national decline. The pattern underscores humanity's desperate need for a perfectly righteous King — fulfilled only in Christ.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The downward spiral of apostasy recorded here serves as a warning to every generation of believers. Sin is never static — it always progresses (James 1:14-15). The remedy is the same in every age: repentance, return to God's Word, and wholehearted dependence on His grace. 'If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us' (1 John 1:9).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVNow the sons of that wicked woman Athaliah had broken into the temple of God and had used even its sacred objects for the Baals.ESVFor the sons of Athaliah, that wicked woman, had broken into the house of God, and had also used all the dedicated things of the house of the LORD for the Baals.NIVTherefore the king summoned Jehoiada the chief priest and said to him, \u201cWhy haven\u2019t you required the Levites to bring in from Judah and Jerusalem the tax imposed by Moses the servant of the Lord and by the assembly of Israel for the tent of the covenant law?\u201dESVSo the king summoned Jehoiada the chief and said to him, \u201cWhy have you not required the Levites to bring in from Judah and Jerusalem the tax levied by Moses, the servant of the LORD, and the congregation of Israel for the tent of testimony?\u201d

rows.label: Verses 1‐14 — Temple Repair Under Jehoiada’s Guidance —
Verses 15‐27 — Apostasy After Jehoiada; Zechariah Murdered;  —

themes.note: Joash — Good Under Jehoiada, Evil After

coaching.tip: After Jehoiada dies, Joash abandons the temple and kills Jehoiada's own son Zechariah when he prophesies against him. The king's faithfulness lasted exactly as long as his mentor's life. Borrowed convictions collapse when the lender dies. Joash had Jehoiada's influence but not Jehoiada's character.
